title: 2018 - HPER Comprehensive Program Review (Part 1)
text:
The Four-Year Cycle and Forms (FALL 2014 – SPRING 2018)
College of the Siskiyous Instructional Program Review
Plan Name – Kinesiology / Health / Physical Education / Athletics
Principal Preparer – Charlie Roche
Contributors – Tim Frisbie
1. Description of Program: Assume the reader doesn’t know anything about your program. Please
describe your program, including the following:
a) *Organization (including staffing and structure)
1 - Associate Dean/Director of Athletics
4 - Fulltime Kinesiology Faculty
2 - Fulltime Athletic Trainers
1- Athletic Coordinator / Eligibility Specialist
1-Fulltime Equipment Specialist
? - Adjunct Faculty
? - Expert Instructional Aids
b) *Primary purpose - The primary purpose of our department is to provide the Northern California
region with intercollegiate athletics, assist students in fulfilling their health and wellness goals, as well as
providing educational requirements for degree and certificate completion. The department continues
to provide exceptional opportunities to students interested in competing in intercollegiate athletics.
Students enrolled in our programs continue to achieve great academic and athletic success, as
evidenced by the percentage of those who matriculate to four-year universities to continue to study and
compete. Additionally, a large majority of our eligible student-athletes receive scholarships.
c) *Whom you serve (including demographics)
We serve a vast amount of students from all different backgrounds. We serve the largest
minority population on the campus.
d) *What kind of services you provide: Intercollegiate Athletics, Health Instruction, Fitness
Instruction, Adaptive Physical Education, Wellness Instruction.
e) *How you provide them (check those that apply)
Non-credit courses, Degree Applicable courses, Transferable courses, Vocational courses, Counseling /
Advising – Provide information on NCAA/NAIA eligibility, Financial Aid – Coaches, Facilities – Coaches
f) *Describe how your curriculum is up-to-date and needs based.
(Base the description on surveys, environmental scan data, transfer patterns, such as GE, IGETC, CSU,
AA-T, or AS-T, accreditation standards, and/or articulation agreements. Consider the results of your
most recent curriculum reviews in this section).
g) *Provide a breakdown of the classes offered in your areas within the Program Review cycle.

-- 1 of 7 --

title: 2018 - HPER Comprehensive Program Review (Part 3)
text:
suggest that a high percentage of our transfer students are students that have taken at least one PEMA
course. Students that take PEMA courses are involved in intercollegiate athletics and are held to very
strict transfer requirements. With the new funding formula that the state has, I see an opportunity for
the PEMA students to be extremely valuable to our campus. I would like to get information on how
many students were recruited for intercollegiate athletics (PEMA) and transfer to a four-year school? I
How many qualify for PELL? How many earn a AA Degree? How many earn an AD-T? I believe with the
new funding model that our intercollegiate athletics (PEMA) program can be extremely successful in
transferring students and degree completion. In fact, I would suggest that we are already the leaders
on campus in doing so.
b) Competition from other institutions – Competition from other institutions has a significant
impact on our PEMA courses. Faculty that teach courses in PEMA are in direct competition with the
majority of the other California community colleges for students. We lack in areas of facilities and
student academic support (academic advisor, athletic counselor, study hall) for the students in PEMA
courses.
c) Requirements of four-year institutions
d) Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates
e) Non-Institutional regulations, policies, standards, and other mandates – The NCAA/NAIA
transfer rules and regulations have a direct impact on the PEMA students. The rules for transfer
students to NCAA/NAIA institutions change often. Our campus relies on our department faculty to know
the transfer rules and regulations. To me this is a function of another office on campus that is not
happening currently. Most students rely on faculty in the department for this information.
f) Job market
Requirements of prospective employers
Development in the field (both current and future)
g) Other
3. Progress on Outcomes Assessment
a) Please summarize the progress your unit has made on program and/or course level SLO
measures you have applied since your last Program Review. We do not currently have a tool in place
to measure program or course level SLO’s. Each faculty member is on their own with no direction from
the college on how to complete this task.
b) Please describe any program/course and/or instructional improvements made by your unit
as a result of the outcomes assessment process. We did replace our program level SLO’s in 2015-16 as
a department. We have talked about a pre-course/post-course fitness assessment for all of our activity
courses. Our lecture courses have SLO assessments built into the course and map directly to the
program level SLO’s. As a department we need some direction on where to go with the activity level
courses and how to collect the data on pre/post course fitness assessment.
c) Please describe the program’s assessment plan for the next four years.

-- 3 of 7 --

title: 2018 - HPER Comprehensive Program Review (Part 4)
text:
Data collection on pre and post fitness assessments for all students enrolled in PEMA, PEFI, and
PEAC activity courses.
4. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators: Please discuss your program’s performance on each
program specific data item provided by the Research Office. If you have already discussed your
program’s performance on one or more of these components then refer to that response here, rather
than repeating it.
a) *Use the data provided by the Research Office to discuss your current Course Completion
Rate and then set a Course Completion Rate goal for your future program review cycle.
b) *Use the data provided by the Research Office to discuss your current Course Success Rate
and then set a Course Success Rate goal for your future Program Review cycle.
c) *Use the data provided by the Research Office to discuss your FT/PT Faculty Ratio and how it
is impacting your program and/or student success.
5. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research: Please provide….
a) *A list of any quantitative or qualitative measures not provided in the previous questions that
you have chosen to gauge your program’s effectiveness (e.g., transfers, degrees, certificates,
satisfaction, student contacts, student headcount, Perkin’s data, equity data, etc.)
b) A summary of the results of these measures.
c) What did you learn from your evaluation of these measures, and what improvements have
you implemented or do you plan to implement as a result of your analysis of these measures?
6. Evaluation: Based upon and not repeating the descriptions you provided in Question 1 and the
responses provided in Questions 2 – 5, please provide an analysis of what is going well and why and
what is not going well and why, in any of the following areas that are relevant to your program:
a) Representativeness of population served
b) Alternative modes and schedules of delivery (e.g., online, hybrid, early morning, evening
services)
c) Partnerships (internal and external)
d) Innovation and implementation of best practices
e) Efficiency in operations
f) Efficiency in resource use
g) Staffing
h) Participation in shared governance (e.g., do unit members feel they participate effectively in
planning and decision-making?)
i) Professional development and training
j) Compliance with applicable mandates

-- 4 of 7 --

title: 2018 - HPER Comprehensive Program Review (Part 5)
text:
7. Vision. Tell us your unit’s vision: Where would you like your program to be four years from now?
Dream big while considering any upcoming changes (e.g., new buildings, labs, growth, changes in the
discipline, etc.)
Kinesiology / Athletics building – Currently we are spread all around campus and we do not have a
sense of department. We do not have dedicated classroom space to teach the lecture portions of our
courses. Our department is often left out in the cold when it comes to classroom scheduling. As faculty
we often have to search for space to teach the lecture portion of some of our courses. If we had a
department building this would provide an area for the lecture portion of our classes to meet in. We
also have several meetings that need to take place with intercollegiate sports teams. There are only two
classrooms on campus large enough to fit some of our programs currently we have very limited access
to both. Where does the largest class on campus (PEMA 1008) have a meeting in a classroom? Our
department is in dire need of this space. We have always just found a way to make it work.
PEMA LABS (Baseball Field, Gymnasium, Football Field, Softball Field, Track, Weight room) – Again some
of the largest classes on our campus and yet our labs poorly maintained or are some of the oldest on
campus. Several colleges that we compete against for students have better PEMA labs than our
campus. The labs for the PEMA courses should be a sense of pride on campus and bring the community
together. There is not a single better recruiting tool that excellent labs for the PEMA courses. With the
weather that we get in our area we need to have better surfaces to teach in. Every PEMA instructor
agrees that we have some of the worst labs in California Community Colleges. Like always we find a way
to make it work. Often time we are embarrassed to show prospective students our labs. We all want
something that we can be extremely proud of. As a campus what value to we put on our PEMA labs? As
a campus where would we rank in comparison to the other colleges we recruit against for students?
Growth – There is an opportunity for growth in the non-credit area for our department. We developed
a PEFI 0519 course. This course has an opportunity to provide many FTE’s to our campus. Our
department needs to find a way to organize this course/program and catch the FTE’s that we are missing
out on every single semester. We believe there is potential for 20-30 FTE that we are missing out on
each semester.
8. Progress on Prior Goals: Briefly summarize any progress your unit has made in meeting the goals and
objectives identified in the program’s last Four-Year Action Plan. One of the goals we had last time was
to re-organize our courses into Kinesiology, PEMA, PEFI, HEA. We have developed two KINE courses and
are in the process of moving our other lecture course from PEMA to KINE. This will bring a more
uniform approach to our departments structure.
9. Four Year-Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal (Goals, Objectives, Resources,
and Actions): Reflect on your responses to all the previous questions. Write a Four-Year Action Plan,
entering the specific program goals you have formulated to maintain or enhance your strengths, or to
address identified challenges. In writing your objectives and developing your resource requests,
consider student learning and program assessment results. Assign an overall priority to each goal and
each objective. In addition, enter any actions and/or resources required to achieve each objective. Then
complete a CQIP for each new budget allocation request.
